5.display: Yes — on one roll you can only get one number, and it cannot be both even and odd.

8.display: No — 6 is itself an even number, so both events can happen at the same time (when you roll a 6).

12.display: No — the King of Hearts is both a King and a Heart, so the events can happen at the same time.

13.display: No, Archer is incorrect — for example, rolling an odd number and rolling an even number on a dice are both mutually exclusive (cannot happen together) and exhaustive (together cover every outcome).

14.display: True — mutually exclusive means the events cannot both happen at once, so the probability of both happening together is 0.

15.display: Not quite — "greater than 3" is {4, 5, 6} and "less than 5" is {1, 2, 3, 4}. They overlap at 4, so they are not mutually exclusive, although together they do cover every outcome (exhaustive).

16.display: The probabilities add up to 1.05, which is more than 1 — for mutually exclusive, exhaustive events, the probabilities must add up to exactly 1.

17.display: Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time (e.g. rolling a 2 and rolling a 5 on one dice roll); exhaustive events cover every possible outcome between them (e.g. rolling an even number or an odd number covers all six outcomes).

18.display: No, Mia is incorrect — 6 is both even and a multiple of 3, so the two events can happen together and are not mutually exclusive.

19.display: Always — if two events cannot happen together, their combined probability P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) can be at most 1, since no probability can exceed 1.

20.display: P(white) = 0.25; number of white chocolates = 15 (0.25 x 60)

26.display: P(none) = 4/5 (0.8); expected tickets winning nothing out of 500 = 400

27.display: P(A) and P(not A) are mutually exclusive (A cannot happen at the same time as not A) and exhaustive (together they cover every possibility), so their probabilities must add to 1 — e.g. P(rain) + P(no rain) = 1.

29.display: For example, a spinner with four sections of probability 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 — these add to exactly 1.

30.display: In real life, weather can be a mixture (e.g. sunny with light rain, or partly cloudy), so the two categories are not always clearly separate — in maths problems we simplify to make the events mutually exclusive for calculation.
